title: The Adventure of the Blanched Soldier
synopsis: James M. Dodd consults Holmes about his friend Godfrey Emsworth, a fellow soldier he served with in the Boer War, who has mysteriously vanished from his family's estate at Tuxbury Old Park. When Dodd visits the estate, he catches a glimpse of a ghostly pale figure — apparently Godfrey — but the family refuses to explain his whereabouts or condition.
solution: Godfrey contracted a skin disease resembling leprosy while in South Africa and has been kept in isolation by his family out of shame and fear; a specialist confirms it is not true leprosy but a closely related condition, ichthyosis, which is treatable, resolving the situation.
notable_elements: One of only two stories narrated by Holmes himself rather than Watson
Holmes opens by praising Watson's usefulness as a sounding board even while gently mocking his literary style
The story hinges on the fear and stigma of leprosy in Edwardian England
Features a medical resolution rather than criminal — no villain, only a frightened family
